<!-- Topic 2: Vector Operations -->
<!-- Slides 12-27 -->

# Vector Operations
<!-- Slide 12 -->

## Changing a Vector {.smaller}

+ How does a vector grow, shrink, and report what it contains?
+ Vector operations change or inspect the container.

::: notes
Slides 12-27
:::

<!-- Slide 13 -->

---

## push_back Adds a Value

`push_back` adds one value to the end of the vector.

```cpp
vector<int> scores;

scores.push_back(88);
scores.push_back(92);
```

The vector now stores `88`, then `92`.

<!-- Slide 14 -->

---

## Execution Trace: push_back

| Statement | scores |
|---|---|
| `vector<int> scores;` | `{ }` |
| `scores.push_back(88);` | `{88}` |
| `scores.push_back(92);` | `{88, 92}` |
| `scores.push_back(75);` | `{88, 92, 75}` |

Each call adds one new value at the end.

<!-- Slide 15 -->

---

## pop_back Removes the Last Value

`pop_back` removes the final value from the vector.

```cpp
vector<int> scores = {88, 92, 75};

scores.pop_back();
```

After this statement, the vector stores `88`, then `92`.

<!-- Slide 16 -->

---

## Interactive Vector Changes

<div class="vector-demo">
<div id="vectorCells0302" class="vector-cells" aria-live="polite"></div>
<div class="vector-buttons">
<button type="button" onclick="pushVector0302()">push_back</button>
<button type="button" onclick="popVector0302()">pop_back</button>
<button type="button" onclick="resetVector0302()">reset</button>
</div>
<p id="vectorStatus0302">vector&lt;int&gt; scores = { };</p>
</div>

<style>
.vector-demo { text-align: center; }
.vector-cells { display: flex; justify-content: center; gap: 0.4rem; min-height: 4rem; margin: 1.2rem 0; }
.vector-cell { border: 3px solid #334155; min-width: 4rem; padding: 0.7rem 0.9rem; font-size: 1.4rem; background: #f8fafc; }
.vector-buttons { display: flex; justify-content: center; gap: 0.6rem; }
.vector-buttons button { font-size: 1rem; padding: 0.45rem 0.8rem; border: 2px solid #334155; background: white; }
</style>

<script>
let vectorValues0302 = [];
let nextVectorValue0302 = 10;
function drawVector0302() {
  const cells = document.getElementById("vectorCells0302");
  const status = document.getElementById("vectorStatus0302");
  if (!cells || !status) return;
  cells.innerHTML = vectorValues0302.map(value => `<div class="vector-cell">${value}</div>`).join("");
  status.textContent = `vector<int> scores = { ${vectorValues0302.join(", ")} };`;
}
function pushVector0302() {
  vectorValues0302.push(nextVectorValue0302);
  nextVectorValue0302 += 10;
  drawVector0302();
}
function popVector0302() {
  vectorValues0302.pop();
  drawVector0302();
}
function resetVector0302() {
  vectorValues0302 = [];
  nextVectorValue0302 = 10;
  drawVector0302();
}
setTimeout(drawVector0302, 0);
</script>

<!-- Slide 17 -->

---

## size Reports the Count

`size` reports how many values are currently stored.

```cpp
vector<int> scores = {88, 92, 75};

cout << scores.size() << endl;
```

This prints `3`.

<!-- Slide 18 -->

---

## Reading the Last Value

The last value is at position `size() - 1`.

```cpp
vector<int> scores = {88, 92, 75};

cout << scores[scores.size() - 1] << endl;
```

This prints `75`.

<!-- Slide 19 -->

---

## Example 1: Daily Temperatures IPO

| Input | Processing | Output |
|---|---|---|
| three temperatures | store each value with `push_back` | number of temperatures |

The vector is the storage choice in the processing step.

<!-- Slide 20 -->

---

## Example 1: Daily Temperatures Code

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    vector<double> temperatures;

    temperatures.push_back(68.5);
    temperatures.push_back(72.0);
    temperatures.push_back(70.5);

    cout << "Days recorded: " << temperatures.size() << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

<!-- Slide 21 -->

---

## Example 1: Execution Trace

| Statement | temperatures | Output |
|---|---|---|
| `vector<double> temperatures;` | `{ }` | |
| `temperatures.push_back(68.5);` | `{68.5}` | |
| `temperatures.push_back(72.0);` | `{68.5, 72.0}` | |
| `temperatures.push_back(70.5);` | `{68.5, 72.0, 70.5}` | |
| `cout << temperatures.size();` | `{68.5, 72.0, 70.5}` | `3` |

The trace makes each vector change visible.

<!-- Slide 22 -->

---

## Example 2: Shopping List

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    vector<string> items = {"eggs", "rice"};

    items.push_back("apples");

    cout << "First item: " << items[0] << endl;
    cout << "Last item: " << items[items.size() - 1] << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

<!-- Slide 23 -->

---

## Example 3: Game Scores

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    vector<int> scores = {100, 80};

    scores.push_back(120);
    scores.push_back(60);
    scores.pop_back();

    cout << "Final score count: " << scores.size() << endl;
    cout << "Last kept score: " << scores[scores.size() - 1] << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

<!-- Slide 24 -->

---

## Operation Summary Table

| Operation | What it does |
|---|---|
| `push_back(value)` | adds a value to the end |
| `pop_back()` | removes the last value |
| `size()` | reports the current count |
| `[index]` | accesses one position |

<!-- Slide 25 -->

---

## Common Practices

+ Use plural names for vectors, such as `scores` or `items`.
+ Check that a vector has values before reading from it.
+ Use `push_back` when the program discovers values one at a time.

<!-- Slide 26 -->

---

## Summary

+ Vector operations can add, remove, count, or access values.
+ `push_back` and `pop_back` change the vector.
+ `size` and index notation help inspect what the vector contains.

<!-- Slide 27 -->
